posts.name: Hardy Wallace
posts.cooked:
Wow. Huge congrats to Pax, Pam and family!

This is a stunning wine. Pax gave me a few last year and said something to the extent of “I think its the best syrah I’ve ever made”…

Bravo!!!
Very exciting to see someone get credit for their hard work.
I tasted this wine last month in Biarritz of all places. The wine is killer. I’d never guess it was CA syrah if tasting it blind. It tastes like a great young cote rotie.

Congrats to Pax!

A 100 from Galloni is a great feather in his cap and at 12.8% it may be one of the lowest alcohol wines to get a 100-point score from any major critic in a long time… (anyone with access to those numbers?)

posts.name: Peter Petersen
posts.cooked:
My subscription to Vinous must have lapsed.
Would definitely be interesting to try that wine.

posts.name: Hardy Wallace
posts.cooked:
Peter Petersen:

My subscription to Vinous must have lapsed.
Would definitely be interesting to try that wine.

posts.cooked:
Eric S n y d e r:

Any idea on aging curve, given no sulfur? I’ll definitely pop one right away, but are these worth aging?

No experience with “sans soufre” Syrah.

The pinnacle of sans soufre Syrah (to me) is allemand sans soufre. It ages quite nice. Had the 2001 twice in 2017 and it was the best Rhone I drank twice in 2017.

posts.name: Peter Petersen
posts.cooked: I have to admit that I’ve never tried Pax as my impression was that these were over the top wines. Now I’m just sorry that I wasn’t on the list so that I could have bought a few Gamays and Hermits as well.

posts.name: David Bueker
posts.cooked: That was the old Pax. The new Pax is bigger than Wind Gap, but not over the top.

posts.name: Al Osterheld
posts.cooked:
The new Pax is definitely not over the top. The new Pax Syrahs are bigger than Wind Gap mainly because of the vineyards, he left the cooler vineyard sites like Majik and Armaugh in the Wind Gap line-up, and makes the wines from Griffin’s Lair, Alder Springs, etc. under the revived Pax label.

-Al

posts.name: Peter Petersen
posts.cooked: Thanks! I’m happy to follow the middle road.

posts.name: Andy K0b3rl
posts.cooked:
D@vid Bu3ker:

That was the old Pax. The new Pax is bigger than Wind Gap, but not over the top.

Great to hear! We had a 2004 Griffin’s Lair recently and were pretty disappointed, lots of overripe fruit and jammy flavors.

Following AG’s score, I’ll give them another try (plus I do like the Wind Gap wines)

posts.name: Adam Altman
posts.cooked: The early 2000s Pax Syrahs were in the ±15% abv range and were quite large. While the new Pax wines are bigger than Wind Gap, they are far from the bombs they used to be.

posts.name: M. Dildine
posts.cooked:
A.Altman:

The early 2000s Pax Syrahs were in the ±15% abv range and were quite large. While the new Pax wines are bigger than Wind Gap, they are far from the bombs they used to be.

Some of those old Pax syrahs still drink really well, surprisingly.

posts.name: Peter Petersen
posts.cooked:
This is definitely a delicious wine. Classic olive and meat aromas, even some earthiness. Nice medium body that is both stylish and elegant.

100pts??? I’ll just do a direct comparison with the '13 Rhys, Horseshoe, Syrah that I drank recently and would rate in the mid 90s. This wine is not quite in the same league as the Rhys, maybe low 90s. That said, I’m really looking forward to drinking them all and buying others from Pax wines in the future. Definitely my style of wine.

posts.name: Al Osterheld
posts.cooked:
I like the Rhys Syrah wines very much and haven’t tried this Pax wine (but, am sure I’d love it). As far as the comparison, note the Pax is quite young compared to the Rhys and will probably evolve over the next three years. Thanks for the note.

-Al

posts.name: Peter Petersen
posts.cooked: Yes, that is a good point about age. Now the Pax is unsulfered and under some sort of fake cork, so while it might age well it is also more of a gamble?

posts.cooked:
Store the Pax well, and it should not be a gamble for the mid-term. Keep it at 55 or lower.

Pretty sure it’s under DIAM, so no worries about the closure.

posts.name: David Bueker
posts.cooked:
Hmm…know much less about those. Oh well, gives me a reason to drink them.

But as for DIAM, I am very confident. I have wines going back a decade in my cellar, and they are perfect. Had an ‘08 ESJ Syrah a few weeks ago that was progressing exactly as I expect ESJ Syrah to do so.

posts.name: Peter Petersen
posts.cooked:
This is not a plastic cork. Made from sugarcane and is recyclable and industrial compostable.
Hopefully this performs better than the various plastic corks from 10-20 years ago.

posts.name: David Bueker
posts.cooked: I know. Still given the way people debate DIAM, which has been doing well for a decade (or more?), the idea that folks should just accept the sugarcane cork is laughable.

posts.name: Hardy Wallace
posts.cooked: and yet we accept cork which has been proven both faulty and inconsistent for 275 years running

posts.name: Scott Watkins
posts.cooked:
H Wallace Jr:

and yet we accept cork which has been proven both faulty and inconsistent for 275 years running

Eh i’m sure people complained when they switched to cork from floating oil on top of wine

posts.name: Peter Petersen
posts.cooked: I’ve no issues with DIAM, except that my Monopol doesn’t work as well on these as on traditional cork.

posts.name: larry schaffer
posts.cooked:
D@vid Bu3ker:

I know. Still given the way people debate DIAM, which has been doing well for a decade (or more?), the idea that folks should just accept the sugarcane cork is laughable.

Agreed. It should be scrutinized just as any other closure is - like screw caps continue to be

One of the ‘coolest’ closures out there, the Vino-Lok, is not scrutinized at all as it pertains to oxygen ingress / ageability . . . I guess if it looks ‘cool enough’, that’s good, right?

posts.name: Peter Petersen
posts.cooked:
Actually I wouldn’t mind if my wines where under Stelvin or glass.

Three corked wines in the last three weeks!

posts.name: larry schaffer
posts.cooked:
Peter Petersen:

Actually I wouldn’t mind if my wines where under Stelvin or glass.

Three corked wines in the last three weeks!

Peter,

Just curious where the studies are that show that wines can age under the glass cork - with the O-ring - for extended periods of time and still show development? I know that you probably don’t have the info - just asking in general . . .

Cheers!

posts.name: David Bueker
posts.cooked:
H Wallace Jr:

and yet we accept cork which has been proven both faulty and inconsistent for 275 years running

I really don’t. I have been favoring screw cap or DIAM finished wines for a decade. Yes, I still buy lots under cork (not happy about that) because it’s what there is.

posts.name: Peter Petersen
posts.cooked:
Larry,
Only personal experience: They don’t go bad over the time periods I expect for what I’ve bought, 5 or so years. Wouldn’t try longer aging given that they seem mostly for wines meant for near term drinking.

Looks like I missed the boat on this one as retail prices on WS are already high. Who’s got a bottle to break off for me?

posts.name: CJ Beazley
posts.cooked:
EHeffner:

Looks like I missed the boat on this one as retail prices on WS are already high. Who’s got a bottle to break off for me?

I got an offer from a local (Dallas) store for 59.99 yesterday.

posts.name: Y@0 C <aitch> 00 ng
posts.cooked:
Popped one today for science; many thanks to Mssr Fu

Double decanted 1h before consumption, glorious lifted floral nose, white pepper, darker fruit, then stern / serious / monolithic / embryonic on the palate and yet curiously pleasurable. Handily outclassed recent vintages of Australian Shiraz and Napa Cab poured alongside (recent vintages; moderately expensive; who the hell cares exactly what/when). A honeyed / brown sugar note emerging over time. Holding the next bottle for ten years down the road. With all due respect to Adrian and others on CT, I don’t see how anyone could consider this to be ‘green’ or ‘thin’. I have no idea what ‘100 points’ even means, but there is great promise in this

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
Science, or curiosity, but I just popped one as well. Came in on Wednesday.

Decanted for an hour.

Reminds me of Halcon mountain fruit, especially Copain Halcon. Like rocks crushed in a glass, grainy mineral texture on the palate. Solid range of fruit expression, from crispy reds and darks and some ripe blue. Really pure fruit. Savoury, broth, saline, white pepper spice and a hint of cured meats. Palate was more alive than the nose, the latter seemingly a bit closed. The acid keeps this ripe wine quite sprite. Chewy but sweet tannins (just a tad drying). I suspect this wine will have a rather long life and drinking window, enjoy now but it will pay much greater dividends with several more years in the bottle.

For my palate, this is an extremely good California syrah. My palate still migrates toward syrah that throws more game, funk, iron and blood, but this wine is a winner.

(93 pts.)

posts.name: Nate Simon
posts.cooked: Don’t know if it’s a regional terroir, winemaking style, or what, but this wine tastes eerily similar to the 2014 Cruse Heintz Syrah, which was also sans soufre. Really nice wine.

RA:
CN: How was it?
RA: I prefer my Syrahs a bit more Frenchified.
CN: Like Todd French?
RA: Northern Rhone.
CN: Yeah that makes more sense.
RA: I like the funk.
CN: That’s probably why you’re palsy with Mollen.
<30 minutes pass>
RA: Send nudes.
CN: I’m assuming that was meant for someone else?
RA: Disregard plz.
CN: You ought to throw a 65 on CellarTracker to f*ck with Fu. He’s planning to use his bottles to pay for his daughter’s education.
RA: I don’t use CT because it doesn’t integrate with Strava. But I’ll give it a 93. For Fu’s sake.
CN: You’ve become the Suckling of Central Florida.

Decanted for an hour.

Reminds me of Halcon mountain fruit, especially Copain Halcon. Like rocks crushed in a glass, grainy mineral texture on the palate. Solid range of fruit expression, from crispy reds and darks and some ripe blue. Really pure fruit. Savoury, broth, saline, white pepper spice and a hint of cured meats. Palate was more alive than the nose, the latter seemingly a bit closed. The acid keeps this ripe wine quite sprite. Chewy but sweet tannins (just a tad drying). I suspect this wine will have a rather long life and drinking window, enjoy now but it will pay much greater dividends with several more years in the bottle.

For my palate, this is an extremely good California syrah. My palate still migrates toward syrah that throws more game, funk, iron and blood, but this wine is a winner.

(93 pts.)

Actual text exchange:

RA:
CN: How was it?
RA: I prefer my Syrahs a bit more Frenchified.
CN: Like Todd French?
RA: Northern Rhone.
CN: Yeah that makes more sense.
RA: I like the funk.
CN: That’s probably why you’re palsy with Mollen.
<30 minutes pass>
RA: Send nudes.
CN: I’m assuming that was meant for someone else?
RA: Disregard plz.
CN: You ought to throw a 65 on CellarTracker to f*ck with Fu. He’s planning to use his bottles to pay for his daughter’s education.
RA: I don’t use CT because it doesn’t integrate with Strava. But I’ll give it a 93. For Fu’s sake.
CN: You’ve become the Suckling of Central Florida.

Corey. Robert’s wine notes/scores are as relevant and sought after as Suckling’s notes on Syrah. I think my daughter’s college fund will be safe.

Look at him, taught Fu everything I know about French syrah, and now that he is all growed up, he shuns me.

Go back to your ripe vintages, Burg Boy! Daddy likes a little stink in his wine.

Suckles does syrah?

posts.name: Ch@rlie F|_|
posts.cooked: That’s my point

• 2016 Pax Syrah Sonoma Hillsides - USA, California, Sonoma County, Russian River Valley (7/15/2018)
    This was my first crack at these wines…and I can see why people love them. There is a depth on the nose that reminds me of N. Rhone…an almost earthy, dirty, rustic wine that’s full of cracked pepper, blackberries, blueberries, and even a hint of “natural insecticide” that’s kind of interesting. This leads from blueberry to cracked pepper, blackberries, bloody meats, and clove on the palate; giving a sense of depth and width in the wine but without the poise & pretension that sometimes might accompany a long finish. I remember a Beer Cellar I used to shop at where the owner would send out daily emails about the new beers and all too often he would include the word “crushable” in an effort to describe the beers that would go down easy…but for this wine it feels almost a perfect descriptor. I will not likely hang onto my other bottles for too long as this wine is too easily “gulp-able”…only 45 minutes in and I’ve already consumed 3/5 of the bottle. Outstanding!!!

Posted from CellarTracker
That label looks hideous

posts.name: Patrick Derdeyn
posts.cooked:
Found it. From the Pax email on the original release of the wine

Pax:

On a recent visit to a retired St.-Joseph legends cellar earlier this year, he gave me a couple of his classic labels, I passed these along to my designer which she made into our own and we bottled this “homage” to him. Extra points if you can name him.
